entry.title: When One Release Has to Reach Many SAP Cloud Integration Tenants
entry.content:
Sharing an internal tool we built for multi-tenant delivery orchestration — and asking whether other teams face the same problem.
If this resonates with enough people, I am open to releasing the source or making this available as a BTP SaaS — but first I want to know whether others face the same problem.

Show the Solution First

The fastest way to explain the solution is to show the interface - One view shows the overview, one view shows the fan-out release across source and target tenants, and one view validates whether a tenant is ready before the first delivery starts. Together, these screens make it clear that the solution is an operator workflow for delivery coordination, not just another technical utility.

When Delivery Becomes a Fan-Out Problem

In our landscape, one release can affect 15 production instances and more than 100 integration artifacts. Before orchestration, a multi-instance production release meant 30+ manual actions across several SAP tools, close to one hour of operator work, and no single place to see the full flow.
 
That is the point where delivery stops being a simple deploy step. It becomes a coordination problem.

Turning Scattered Steps into One Governed Flow

SAP Cloud Transport Management(TMS) already handled transport and routing. Content Agent Service(CAS) works for exporting MTAR files, i.e., automatically generating Transport Requests. SAP Cloud Integration already handled deployment and runtime operations. The gap was the operator workflow across many targets.
 
To close that gap, we built an internal orchestration layer we call Delivery Orchestrator for Cloud Integration. It coordinates request creation, route-aware target calculation, batch import, batch deploy, centralized status, approvals, notifications, and traceability in one place. It does not replace SAP tools. It makes them usable as one governed release flow.
 
For our team, that changed one multi-instance release from 30+ manual actions and about one hour into a 4-step flow that takes about five minutes. Just as importantly, approval and auditability moved into the workflow instead of being handled around it.
The clearest way to understand that change is the Delivery Flow view itself. One screen shows the source tenant, the target tenants, the transport path, and the import and deploy state together. For an operator, that is the difference between understanding a release and chasing it across multiple tools.

Bootstraper - Reliable Fan-Out Starts Before the First Import

Once fan-out delivery became easier, another weakness became impossible to ignore: tenant bootstrap. Connecting a new SAP Cloud Integration tenant still depended on destinations, service instances, authentication settings, and other BTP prerequisites being configured correctly. The hard part was not the manual setup itself. The hard part was that misconfiguration often remained invisible until the first real import or deploy failed.
 
That changes the meaning of bootstrap. In a one-to-many delivery model, bootstrap is not just onboarding. It is part of delivery readiness.
 
This is why guided tenant bootstrap and prerequisite validation became an important extension of the orchestration story for us. The goal is to inspect what is missing, make setup visible, automate what can be done safely, and surface what still requires manual governance. Instead of discovering a wrong URL, missing token, or incomplete service setup during runtime delivery, the operator can see those gaps before the first release starts.
 

A Secondary Capability: Version Compare

Bootstrap is the more important next step for us, because reliable onboarding is what makes the product safer to adopt across teams. But once many tenants are active, another operational question quickly matters as well: what is actually different across them?
 
That is where Version Compare becomes useful as a secondary capability. It gives operators a cross-tenant view of design-time and runtime artifact versions, highlights mismatches, and makes it easier to see where source and target tenants are no longer aligned. It is not the main story of this article, and it does not matter more than bootstrap. But it is still valuable because operational reliability is not only about moving releases forward. It is also about spotting drift early enough to act on it. 

 

A Question to the Community

We are sharing this pattern now because the core flow is already live and internally validated. The goal is not to announce a finished product, but to learn whether other SAP Cloud Integration teams face the same combination of fan-out delivery, bootstrap fragility, and cross-tenant drift.

If you operate across multiple tenants, I would be very interested to hear:
  • how you coordinate one release across many targets today
  • how much manual work still sits between transport and deployment
  • whether tenant bootstrap or prerequisite validation becomes a bottleneck in your landscape
  • how you currently see version drift across tenants

entry.title: Unexpected plugin behaviour of Data collection plugins in POD 2.0 framework in SAP DM
entry.content:
Hi Community Members,

We are currently working with Data Collection related plugins in POD 2.0 and have observed several inconsistencies and limitations in the Data Collection Group table and POD behavior. I would like to highlight these points to understand whether this is standard behavior, a configuration gap, or a limitations of new 2.0 framework.

Below are the detailed observations.

1. Postings Button – No Clear Purpose

In the Data Collection Group table, a Postings button is available.

However:

  • The button does not seem to have any functional significance.
  • There is no mention or explanation of this button in the SAP Help Documentation.
  • Clicking the button does not result in any meaningful or expected action (at least from a functional standpoint).

This raises questions such as:

  • What is the intended purpose of the Postings button?

  • Is it meant for a future enhancement, or is it currently unused?

2. Collect Button Enabled Even When Multiple Data Collection = False

We observed that:

  • The Collect button is enabled even for Data Collection Groups where:
    • Multiple Data Collection = False
    • Data has already been collected one time
  • The button behavior is not configurable
  • From a usability point of view, the button should ideally be:
    • Disabled, or
    • Hidden once data collection is completed for non‑multiple data collection groups (the usual POD 1.0 behaviour)

This can be misleading for users and may result in unnecessary button clicks and errors.


3. Unable to View Collected Parameters from POD

Another issue we are facing:

  • After data collection is completed, the collected parameters are not visible from the POD
  • There seems to be no direct way for users to:
    • Review collected values
    • Verify what was captured during data collection ( Which we used to do in POD 1.0)

Clarification needed:

  • Is there a standard POD plugin or configuration required to display collected parameters?

  • Or is this a current limitation of the framework?

4. Data Collection Groups Refresh Action Not getting Triggered via Custom action Button

We attempted to improve usability by:

  • Assigning the Data Collection Refresh action to a custom button where we are collecting data using a production process design in the POD

However:

  • Clicking the custom button does trigger the PPD but not the refresh action (confirmed this by checking in Network tab as well, where we didn't saw and call happened for DC groups refresh)
  • The action works only when using standard/default "save button" that they have given along with the Workcenter POD template in the Data collection parameter plugin.

This limits flexibility in POD customization and user‑driven refresh behavior.

 

Are these known limitations or expected system behaviors?

Are there recommended workarounds or best practices?

Is there any roadmap or note addressing these issues?

Any guidance or insights from SAP experts or peers would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance.
Sudheer Manepalli

entry.title: Re: Error AAPO105
entry.content: No, you cannot enable valuation area 12 only for one AUC asset. It is either global (AFBN) or you create a new asset master with the correct areas. Having said that the Best Practice is if valuation area 12 is required for reporting or statutory purposes, the clean approach is to activate it globally via AFBN. If it is only needed for a subset of assets, then the safer path is to create new assets with the correct valuation areas and transfer values.

entry.title: Re: Creation of New PO condition Type
entry.content: Create a new condition type in customizing, mark it as statistical and set an Accrual Key, so it does not change the material price, add it to the PO pricing schema, and then assign a separate G/L account in account determination. This way, the PO condition posts independently to its own account without affecting inventory valuation.

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP Watch List Screening – Delta Screening & Streamlined Investigation
entry.content:
With advance in technology, also comes increased expectations from regulatory bodies for companies to rapidly adapt to changing circumstances. For instance, when it comes to sanctioned party screening, authorities now prioritize real-time compliance, contextual analysis and enhanced due diligence processes over ad-hoc list-matching.

To continuously support organizations in this area, multiple enhancements have once again been delivered to SAP Watch List Screening.

In this short blog, I have selected a few, but you will also find more details in the dedicated section of the SAP Help Portal: What’s New in SAP Watch List Screening

 

Delta Screening

 

If there is no predetermined timetable for sanction lists update but rather names and entities being added or removed by authorities as necessary and appropriate, one thing is sure: this is quite frequent with some lists being modified as regularly as daily. Not only to add or remove records, but to include additional details, aliases, updated addresses, etc.

And, if experts assess that the percentage of these updates is limited (some claim less than 1% of total records are updated daily), this still means a great volume of changes that organizations need to remain compliant with.

To do so, compliance departments can opt for 2 options:

  • Systematic full rescreening of all records and entire sanction list set => this is the “bullet proof” approach – or at least as close as it gets of course, but equates in practice to a new full screening at every run so it is heavy and not efficient for ongoing monitoring
  • Delta screening with targeted compliance measures that re-check only newly added or updated watch list records for potential sanction hits => with this approach, Compliance Officers benefit from faster, more focused checks, saving time and ensuring that newly added or updated data is promptly screened without missing potential risks

Full screening was already available in SAP Watch List Screening since the very inception of this service where documents were re-checked upon changes and further processing and business partners had an own scheduling report for full re-screenings.

Delta Screening has been added in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Edition 2602 and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise release 2025 FPS2. With Delta Screening, the changes in watch lists are monitored and trigger a screening re-run on customer information - request status, business partners, and documents.

Allow me to illustrate this with a simple example from the SAP Help Portal section for this enhancement:

A business partner is screened on creation, resulting in no hits. Therefore, the business partner is released. Some days later, a new watch list version is uploaded containing new watch list entries. SAP Watch List Screening now automatically screens the business partner against these new watch list entries and finds a hit and blocks the business partner. The next postprocessing run fetches this update and blocks the business partner in the system accordingly. In doing so, the screening status of the business partner (as well as the documents) stays up-to-date with watch list changes, ensuring consistency and compliance.

With delta screening, companies can:

 

 

Audit Trail

 

Full auditability of sanction party list screenings is often a legal requirement within sanction regulations. For instance, under 31 CFR Part 501 - Records and recordkeeping requirements, the U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) mandates that organizations “keep a full and accurate record of each such transaction engaged in”.

Complete logs of who performed the screening, what was screened, and when it took place help businesses accomplish just that: demonstrate compliance, manage liability, and foster trust by showing that processes are transparent and defensible.

To support this requirement, an new Fiori app has been released: Display Audit Trail.

With this app organizations – or their auditors – can display an audit trail for their screening requests and navigate to individual screening requests for further investigation:

 

 

Display Watch Lists

 

Following on the topic of audit, you may recall that we previously mentioned in a What’s new in SAP Watch List Screening blog the availability of Custom Watch Lists that organizations can upload in addition to the provided list.

Whether it be for predefined provided lists or custom lists, auditors, but also compliance managers and screening specialists have asked for the ability to access the details of the lists:

 

Auditors

To audit and verify that the correct watch lists are in use

 

Compliance managers

To assess the completeness and coverage of screening content and verify their custom lists post-upload

 

Screening specialists

For support during an investigation to understand why certain names are flagged and pre-emptively analyse watch list contents

 

Here again, to help customers, a new Fiori app has been released: Display Watch Lists

With this app users can display all entries of the watch lists they are using:

 

 

Link back to original watch list source

 

Finally, and to close this blog on investigation by screening specialists, when a hit is generated, these experts need to verify if the associated entity is truly the one listed on the sanction party list. To do so, they often need to refer back to the original documentation – list from OFAC, United Nations, European Union, country specific… and many more! – where all attributes and comments are detailed, to ensure they enforce the right decision.

With the new capability to link back to the original watch list source, this step of the investigation is streamlined and handling time per alert due to manual searching reduced:

What about you, how does your company address these needs of constantly changing sanction lists and complex investigations? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Budget Availability Control for WBS Element in PR/PO...
entry.content:
Some of the options / Best Practices in Public Cloud

1) Custom Logic (BAdI)
You can implement a Business Add‑In (BAdI) in the Purchase Requisition or Purchase Order creation process. Logic can check the WBS budget consumption and raise a warning or error message. This is the closest equivalent to AVC in public cloud.

2) Workflow‑based Validation
Configure a workflow approval step that triggers when PR/PO exceeds budget. This does not block creation but prevents release until approved.

3) Alternative Objects (Internal Orders)
If strict budget control is required, some customers use internal orders with budget checks (supported in cloud). Internal orders can enforce budget availability more tightly than WBS in public cloud.

entry.title: Re: My Purchase Requisition becomes Advance license
entry.content:
@jozdzinski I am really happy to hear that 🙂

Yes, you are right, these changes can be confusing and difficult to understand.

During my IAM training from SAP Consulting I was told that the smartest way is to not add all dependent apps. Instead, I am always starting with just the specific app I need and then run a test if the process works as expected.

  • If yes, then I am happy and I do not add the additional apps.
  • If no, then I perform an analysis (by checking via app “Display Authorization Audit”) and add only the dependent app that I am specifically missing.

 

Such changes can be tracked in advance by using tools like the What’s New Viewer (What's New Viewer - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal) or RASD (Release Assessment and Scope Dependency for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition) as described here: Key Tools and Resources for IAM Upgrade & Change Management | SAP Help Portal.

However, I have felt in recent releases that I can not find all changes here. So it’s best to just go through the system with heightened sensed in the weeks after a release.

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: Wiosenne Spotkanie Katowickiej Grupy SAP Community - Kwiecień 2026
entry.content:
Relacja ze spotkania SAP Stammtisch – Katowice, 15.04.2026

Kwietniowe spotkanie katowickiego SAP Stammtisch po raz kolejny pokazało, że siła tej inicjatywy tkwi w ludziach i otwartej wymianie doświadczeń. 15 kwietnia spotkaliśmy się w sprawdzonym gronie, ale co szczególnie cieszy – dołączyły do nas również trzy nowe osoby, które od razu wniosły świeżą perspektywę i energię do dyskusji.

Już od pierwszych minut było widać, że nie będzie to „zwykłe” spotkanie. Rozmowy szybko zeszły na konkretne, techniczne tematy – od podejścia do clean code w projektach SAP, przez praktyczne doświadczenia z SAP BTP, aż po realne wyzwania, które pojawiają się podczas wdrożeń. To właśnie ten miks - teorii, praktyki i szczerych opinii z projektów - sprawia, że Stammtisch ma swoją unikalną wartość.

Nie zabrakło również dyskusji o tym, co zmienia się w świecie SAP na bieżąco. Uczestnicy dzielili się obserwacjami dotyczącymi rozwoju produktów, zmian w podejściu do architektury oraz kierunków, w jakich zmierza ekosystem SAP. Takie rozmowy często wykraczają poza „oficjalną dokumentację” i pozwalają spojrzeć na technologie z zupełnie innej, bardziej praktycznej strony.

Szczególnie wartościowy był fakt, że nowe osoby nie tylko słuchały, ale aktywnie uczestniczyły w rozmowach zadając pytania, dzieląc się własnymi doświadczeniami i wnosząc nowe wątki. To najlepszy dowód na to, że SAP Stammtisch jest miejscem otwartym niezależnie od poziomu doświadczenia czy roli w projektach, gdzie wszyscy uczestnicy chętnie ze sobą rozmawiają.

Co dalej?

Nie zwalniamy tempa! Kolejne spotkanie zapowiada się równie ciekawie i odbędzie się już 24.06.2026.

Planujemy kontynuować tematykę AI w SAP, ze szczególnym naciskiem na SAP Joule, który budzi coraz większe zainteresowanie i rodzi sporo pytań w kontekście praktycznego wykorzystania.

Dodatkowo na tapetę trafi bardzo aktualny temat:

  • zmiany w API Policy (v4 2026) i ich wpływ na istniejące integracje klientów

To zagadnienie już teraz wywołuje sporo dyskusji w społeczności i z pewnością będzie okazją do wymiany konkretnych doświadczeń oraz potencjalnych strategii dostosowania się do nowych zasad.

Dlaczego warto przyjść?

Bo to nie jest konferencja ani webinar trzymający się sztywnych ram i wąskiej tematyki.
To miejsce, gdzie:

  • możesz swobodnie pogadać o realnych problemach projektowych
  • poznasz ludzi z różnych obszarów SAP
  • dowiesz się, jak inni rozwiązują podobne wyzwania
  • i co najważniejsze, poszerzysz nowe kontakty

Każde kolejne spotkanie pokazuje, że społeczność rośnie, a wraz z nią rośnie poziom i różnorodność dyskusji.

Jeśli jeszcze nie byłeś – to dobry moment, żeby dołączyć.
Jeśli byłeś – wiesz, że warto wrócić! 🙂

entry.title: Re: App or tool for massive upload condititons material i...
entry.content:
In S/4HANA Public Cloud, the recommended way is to use the Data Migration Cockpit (Fiori app “Migrate Your Data”). If inclusion/exclusion conditions are part of a custom condition table, you may need a custom migration object.

In Public Cloud, you can use SAP standard APIs (OData services) to create/update condition records programmatically. This is often the best option if you need recurring mass uploads.

entry.title: Authorization Model in SAP Logistics Management
entry.content:
If you have ever set up SAP Logistics Management for a new customer, you've probably encountered the question: "My user can log in, but they see no data — or worse, nothing at all." Authorization in LGM has a few moving parts, and understanding how they fit together makes all the difference between a smooth go-live and a frustrating troubleshooting session. This post walks through how LGM authorization works, what to configure, and how to diagnose the most common issues.

The Big Picture: Two Layers of Authorization

LGM uses a dual authorization model. There are two independent layers that must both be configured correctly:

  • Role Collections — managed in the BTP Cockpit. These control UI visibility: which app tiles, spaces, and integration cards a user sees in SAP Build Work Zone (BWZ).
  • AMS Policies — managed in SAP Cloud Identity Services (SCI). These control data access: which records a user can read or change, enforced at every public API endpoint (UI, Public API, SAP Internal).

A common mistake is configuring only one of these layers. If a user has a Role Collection but no AMS Policy, they can see the app tiles but get a 403 error when the app tries to load data. If they have an AMS Policy but no Role Collection, the launchpad may show nothing at all. Both layers are required.

Important change as of December 2025: Before November 2025, LGM did not enforce AMS Policies. Customers who onboarded during Beta or RTC only configured Role Collections and everything worked. Starting with the December 2025 release, AMS enforcement is active. If your users suddenly lost access after an upgrade, this is the most likely reason.

The Technology Behind It: SAP Cloud Identity Services

LGM uses SAP Cloud Identity Services (SCI), which bundles two capabilities:

  • IAS (Identity Authentication Service) — handles authentication (who you are). It issues tokens, manages federation via SAML or OIDC, and connects your corporate identity provider to the SAP ecosystem.
  • AMS (Authorization Management Service) — handles authorization (what you can do). It stores policies and roles, and injects authorization conditions into every data request at runtime.

When a user clicks a tile and LGM loads, a token exchange happens behind the scenes: the BWZ token is exchanged for an LGM token that carries the user's AMS policies. The CAP framework then evaluates those policies on every data access — automatically, without any custom code needed in the application.

How the Request Flow Works

Understanding the flow helps a lot when diagnosing problems:

  1. The user opens the BWZ site and IAS authenticates them.
  2. BWZ checks the user's Role Collections and shows (or hides) tiles accordingly.
  3. The user clicks a tile. LGM loads and a token exchange occurs.
  4. The CAP service receives the first request, calls the AMS server to fetch the user's roles and attribute restrictions, and caches these for the session.
  5. Every subsequent data request is filtered automatically: only records matching the user's allowed warehouses and sites are returned.

One practical consequence of step 4: policy changes do not take effect during an active session. If you assign a new policy to a user, they must fully log out (closing all browser tabs) and log back in before the change takes effect. This is a common source of confusion during troubleshooting — "I assigned the policy but nothing changed."

The Base Policies: Who Gets What

LGM ships with six SAP-delivered base policies that cover the most common user personas:

  • Logistics Superuser — full access to all LGM functions except Stock Upload. No instance restrictions. Intended for power users, administrators, and support staff.
  • Logistics Clerk — the most commonly used policy for dispatchers, transportation buyers, and warehouse clerks. Covers a broad set of operations: Warehouse Requests, Warehouse Tasks, Stock, Handling Units, Packing, Transportation Requests, Consignments, Pickup/Delivery Documents, and more. Warehousing roles are restricted by the Warehouse attribute; transportation roles (Transportation Requests, Consignments) currently have no instance-based restriction.
  • Warehouse Operative — for pickers, packers, and receivers who need to process tasks on the shop floor. Access to Warehouse Task processing, Stock display, Packing, and Handling Units. Restricted to specific warehouses.
  • Business Process Specialist — for users responsible for business configuration. Covers business rules, organizational model, shipping processes, consolidation cutoff, pickup schedules, stock reconciliation, business automations, and related master data. Warehousing-related roles are restricted by the Warehouse attribute.
  • Administrator — for IT roles managing technical configuration and S/4 integration: replication schedules, system connections, organizational model, and unit of measure. No instance restrictions.
  • Stock Upload — a temporary policy for initial inventory uploads. Should be removed once the initial stock is loaded. Restricted by the Warehouse attribute.

For most implementations, the right starting point is: assign Logistics Clerk (with appropriate warehouse restrictions) to your operational users, Warehouse Operative to shop-floor workers, Business Process Specialist to configuration managers, and Administrator to your IT team. Add Logistics Superuser sparingly — only for users who genuinely need unrestricted access. Use Stock Upload only during initial system setup, then revoke it.

Instance Restrictions: Scoping Access by Warehouse or Site

One of the most powerful features of LGM authorization is instance-based access control. Rather than creating separate roles for each warehouse, you assign the same policy (e.g., Logistics Clerk) to all users but restrict each user to the specific warehouses or sites they should see.

This works as follows:

  • The AMS Policy defines allowed Warehouse or Site values for the user.
  • The CAP framework automatically injects a WHERE condition into every data query — for example, WHERE warehouse_displayId IN ('WH01', 'WH02').
  • Requests for records outside the allowed scope return empty results, not a 403 error. This is intentional: a 403 would reveal that the data exists; empty results are safer.

If a user reports "I can open the app but I don't see any data," the first thing to check is whether their AMS Policy includes the correct Warehouse or Site attribute values.

Role Collections and the BWZ Launchpad

Role Collections are managed in the BTP Cockpit under your subaccount's Security settings. When LGM is configured as a BWZ Content Provider, six Role Collections are created automatically:

  • LGM Logistics Superuser
  • LGM Logistics Clerk
  • LGM Warehouse Operative
  • LGM Business Process Specialist
  • LGM Administrator
  • LGM Stock Upload

Each Role Collection maps to the corresponding AMS base policy. In general, the Role Collection you assign in BTP should match the AMS Policy you assign in IAS. Mismatches — for example, giving a user the "LGM Logistics Clerk" Role Collection but the "Logistics Superuser" AMS Policy — lead to confusing behavior where the UI shows a limited set of tiles but the backend returns more data than expected.

Note: Role Collections only affect the BWZ launchpad. Users accessing LGM via the DwC launchpad or directly through the API are not subject to Role Collection checks — only AMS Policies apply to them.

Diagnosing Authorization Issues: A Step-by-Step Checklist

When a user reports an access problem, work through these checks in order:

  1. Can the user authenticate? If they hit a redirect loop or "Identity provider not found," the IAS Trust configuration in BTP is missing or misconfigured.
  2. Does the launchpad show tiles? If the launchpad is completely empty, either the BWZ Content Provider hasn't been deployed or no Role Collection has been assigned. If some tiles are missing, the Role Collection is incomplete or the wrong one was assigned.
  3. Can the user open an app? If clicking a tile results in an error, the Role Collection may be missing the required entry, or the BWZ Content deployment is incomplete.
  4. Does the app show data? If the app loads but shows no records, the AMS Policy is missing. This is the most common issue for customers who onboarded before December 2025.
  5. Does the user see the correct subset of data? If data is visible but wrong warehouses or sites appear (or are missing), check the Warehouse/Site attribute values in the AMS Policy.

The Most Common Mistakes

Here are the issues that come up most often in support:

"I only assigned a Role Collection."
This was sufficient before December 2025. It is no longer sufficient. Both a Role Collection and an AMS Policy are required. If your users lost access after a recent upgrade, assign the matching AMS Policy in IAS.

"I assigned the policy but the user still has the same problem."
Authorization claims are cached for the session. The user must log out of all browser tabs and log back in before new policy assignments take effect.

"I deleted an IAS group that started with 'Base -'."
Groups prefixed with "Base -" are system-managed by AMS and must not be deleted. They are automatically created for each base policy and link users to their policy entitlements. If one has been deleted, the policy mapping is broken and must be restored by contacting SAP Support.

"Some integration cards show 500 errors."
If all cards show 500 errors (not 403), the most common cause is browser cookie settings. The "Prevent cross-site tracking" feature in Safari and some other browsers blocks the cross-site cookies that integration cards depend on. Disabling this setting or using a different browser resolves the issue.

"The user can access data through the API but not through the DwC launchpad."
This typically indicates a broken "user chain" in the DwC integration. Escalate to the development team with the correlation ID from the failing request.

A Practical Setup Checklist

For a new user, the complete configuration requires four steps across three systems:

  1. BTP Cockpit — Trust Configuration: Ensure IAS is configured as a trusted identity provider in the BTP subaccount.
  2. BTP Cockpit — Role Collections: Assign the appropriate LGM Role Collection to the user (e.g., "LGM Logistics Clerk").
  3. IAS Admin Console — AMS Policy: Assign the matching base policy to the user (e.g., "Logistics Clerk"). Set Warehouse or Site attribute values to restrict their data scope.
  4. BWZ Site Manager — Content Deployment: Confirm that LGM content has been deployed to the BWZ site.

After completing all four steps, have the user log in fresh. Verify with both a positive test (can they access the data they should?) and a negative test (are they blocked from data they shouldn't see?).

Wrapping Up

LGM authorization has more layers than most SAP applications, but once you understand the model it becomes predictable. The dual layer — Role Collections for UI visibility, AMS Policies for data access — gives you precise control over what each user sees and can do. Instance-based restrictions let you reuse the same base policies across a multi-warehouse environment without creating custom roles for every location.

Key takeaways:

  • Both a Role Collection (BTP) and an AMS Policy (IAS) are required since December 2025.
  • Policy changes only take effect after a full logout and re-login.
  • "No data visible" almost always means the AMS Policy is missing or the Warehouse/Site attribute is not set.
  • Never delete IAS groups prefixed with "Base -".
  • For DwC users, only AMS Policies apply — Role Collections are BWZ-only.

Have you run into an authorization scenario in your LGM implementation that doesn't fit neatly into the model described here? Share your experience in the comments — there are likely others who have hit the same edge case, and the community benefits from hearing about it.

entry.title: Re: Getting error while creating Asset master : Area 01 i...
entry.content:
Why it happens is because, Area 01 is the leading depreciation area (book depreciation). In your chart of depreciation, Area 01 is not flagged as a revaluation area. However, your asset class or asset master template is set up to expect revaluation postings in Area 01. The system therefore prompts you to maintain Area 01 as a revaluation area, even though that is not normally correct.

Check your asset class (OAOA) and depreciation area (OAYZ) settings. the solution is to correct the dependency, not to force Area 01 into revaluation.

entry.title: Re: Calculate Average Salary per Job level using data action
entry.content:
@Manjunath9 :
Please try the below. You can replace variable member with real measurses if you are inetersted in intermediate results for Count and Total.

MEMBERSET [d/Date] = "202712"
MEMBERSET [d/Measures] = "Amount_LC"
MEMBERSET [d/Job_Level] = ("JL1","JL2")   // Restrict your Job Level
MEMBERSET [d/CostCenter] = ("CC100","CC200","CC300","CC400","CC500") // Restrict your CC here 

VARIABLEMEMBER #COUNT OF [d/Measures]
VARIABLEMEMBER #TOTAL OF [d/Measures]

DATA([d/Measures]=#COUNT,[d/CostCenter] = "#") = RESULTLOOKUP()*0 + 1

DATA([d/Measures]=#TOTAL,[d/CostCenter] = "#") = RESULTLOOKUP()

DATA([d/Measures]="Average") =
    RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures]=#TOTAL,[d/CostCenter]="#")
    /
    RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures]=#COUNT,[d/CostCenter]="#")

entry.title: Re: How to check the memory usage of a specific table in...
entry.content:
Column tables:

SELECT TABLE_NAME AS "Table",
round(MEMORY_SIZE_IN_TOTAL/1024/1024, 2) as "MB"
FROM M_CS_TABLES
WHERE SCHEMA_NAME = 'YOUR_SCHEMA'
AND TABLE_NAME = 'YOUR_TABLE_NAME'
ORDER BY "MB" DESC;

Row tables:

SELECT SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME,
round((USED_FIXED_PART_SIZE + USED_VARIABLE_PART_SIZE)/1024/1024, 2) AS "MB Used"
FROM M_RS_TABLES
WHERE SCHEMA_NAME = 'YOUR_SCHEMA'
AND TABLE_NAME = 'YOUR_TABLE_NAME'
ORDER BY "MB Used" DESC;

entry.title: Re: How to create the DO from old Delivery Schedule in Sa...
entry.content:
Good day @NEO1 

Thank you for your detailed scenario and screenshots.

For the case you described, the simple answer is that you cannot create an outbound delivery from an old JIT delivery schedule version in the sales scheduling agreement.

In the standard sales scheduling agreement process, a new delivery schedule replaced the previous delivery schedule, either fully or partly, for the relevant date and quantity. Also, JIT delivery schedule takes precedence over the forecast delivery schedule within the JIT horizon.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/e5c284520bd741968fb9c4768e91cc21.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/cd256a63aba744f48cca682610f6fbeb.html?locale=en-US

KBA 3253952 also explains that old schedule lines are removed from the active delivery schedule when a new delivery schedule is created. You can still view the old schedule lines as historical schedule lines by using the Analysis button, but they are no longer the active basis for delivery creation.

So, in your example, after Version 3 is created, Version 3 is the current active JIT delivery schedule. Versions 1 and 2 are available only as historical information. You can display and compare them, but you cannot select them as delivery sources in the delivery due list.

See the following reference for more details.

2802216 - Link between a scheduling agreement's schedule lines and deliveries - SAP for Me

My recommendation is, if the busines wants to deliver against the older customer requested quantities or dates, the standard approach is not to create the delivery from the old version. Instead, the busines should make the required quantity and date active again in the current delivery schedule.

The following options are available:

Option 1, ask the customer to send a new JIT delivery schedule containing the required old dates and quantities. Basically, the customer sends a new JIT delivery schedule, and the system creates a new current version. Then the delivery due list will pick up those active schedule lines.

Option 2, if allowed in your process, maintain the required schedule line date and quantity again in the current JIT delivery schedule. After it becomes part of the current active delivery schedule, the delivery list can select it.

Option 3, is to use Analysis or Display releases to compare old and current version. You will be able to investigate and audit, but it does not make the old version delivery relevant again.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

Support Request Template: GDPR-Deleted Person Data Retrieval

Subject Title: Temporary Data Recovery for GDPR-Deleted Records

Prerequisites Required Before Support Engagement:

• Specific record Ids (Technician/User ID) that need temporary recovery
• Legal/compliance approval for GDPR data access
• Clear definition of required data fields and retention period

Record Details:

  • UnifiedPerson IDs: [List specific IDs] (FSM Only table)
  • From the Query API the email address, first and last name will be visible

entry.title: Re: Deactivate the use of Reference Exchange Rate Indicat...
entry.content:
Hi JAS3311,

It is not possible to deactivate the reference exchange rate in the C/T method. It is there on purpose to ensure that what is balanced in local currency remains balanced in group currency.

About your statement: "we want the B/S items to be translated using the CLOsing Exchange Rate, and the P/L items via the AVERage Exchange Rate."

This is exactly what the C/T methods like S0903 or Y0901 do (with an exception for investments and equity FS items, that you can easily adjust if needed).

For B/S items, the closing balance is translated using closing rate, but the movements (period values) are translated using the average rate. This is needed to ensure the P&L and B/S consistency in the financial statements. Also, the net income in the B/S is translated using the average rate (again, to be consistent with the P&L), this is what triggers the C/T difference on 314800.

You can find more details here: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/90c07e91c7a64f328be3fd6b48955b13/1eac9a4436204081956213fb2d265094.html?version=LATEST&locale=en-US

Hope this helps.

Best regards,

Grégoire

entry.title: Fixing Mobile Approvals in SAP S/4HANA Cloud: Why “Open Task” Needs to Go
entry.content:
In today’s mobile‑first enterprise, executives and managers expect to approve critical business transactions anytime, anywhere—often straight from their smartphones. However, a design limitation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is creating unnecessary friction for mobile users, particularly when approving payment requests through My Inbox (SAP Fiori).

This blog explores the challenge, its real business impact, and why enabling direct access to approval details from notification emails on mobile devices is a small change that could deliver significant value.

This is exactly why the new SAP Influence idea
“Enable Direct Access to Approval Details from Notification Email on Mobile Devices in My Inbox”
(described in Enable Direct Access to Approval Details from Notification Email on Mobile Devices in My Inbox ) is so critical.

The Problem: Too Many Steps for a Simple Approval for managers including C-level leaderships

Currently, when an approver receives a notification email for a payment request and clicks the link on a mobile device, the experience is far from intuitive:

  1. The user opens the notification email
  2. Clicks the embedded link
  3. Lands only on the My Inbox task list
  4. Must locate and tap Open Task
  5. Click again to finally reach the approval details
  6. Click … to show button Approve and Submit to finally approve it

This flow may sound manageable on a desktop, but on mobile devices it quickly becomes frustrating.

Why?

  • The Open Task button is hard to find on small screens
  • Browser limitations or authorization issues can block access
  • SAP has officially confirmed that Open Task is not supported on mobile devices by design

As a result, many approvals simply don’t get completed while on the go.

Confirmed: This Is a Product Limitation, Not Configuration

SAP Support has explicitly confirmed in the case that this behavior is a design limitation of My Inbox, not a customer setup issue.

In short:

Mobile users are required to use a function that is not supported on mobile.

This leaves customers with no workaround—only delays.

Business Impact: Small UX Issue, Big Operational Risk

For finance leaders, approval delays are not just inconvenient—they can be costly.

Real‑world impact includes:

  • Delayed payment approvals
  • Slower vendor settlements
  • Reduced adoption of mobile approvals
  • Increased reliance on desktop access
  • Approval bottlenecks during travel or remote work

For roles such as Finance Managers, CFOs, CEOs, and senior executives, approvals are often time‑critical. When mobile approvals fail, they directly affect:

  • Cash flow
  • Vendor relationships
  • Operational continuity

What Users Actually Expect

Modern enterprise applications should behave intelligently based on context—especially device type.

Expected behavior is simple:

  • When a user taps an approval link from an email on mobile
  • The system should open the approval detail screen directly
  • The Open Task step should be bypassed
  • Navigation should be fast, intuitive, and reliable

This is not about changing business logic; it’s about better user experience.

A Low‑Risk, High‑Value Enhancement for SAP

From a product perspective, this enhancement offers significant upside with minimal risk.

Potential solution approaches SAP could consider:

  • Deep links that open the approval object directly
  • Context‑aware navigation that skips “Open Task” on mobile
  • Mobile‑optimized approval screens for inbox items
  • Intent‑based Fiori navigation parameters

None of these options alter approval logic or security—they only streamline access.

Why This Matters for SAP’s Cloud Strategy

This request aligns perfectly with SAP’s stated direction:

  • ✅Mobile‑first design
  • ✅SAP Fiori UX improvements
  • ✅Cloud simplicity and automation
  • ✅Executive‑ready digital experiences

By addressing this issue, SAP would:

  • Improve mobile usability of My Inbox
  • Increase mobile approval adoption
  • Reduce customer support cases
  • Strengthen SAP’s executive mobile experience

Final Thoughts: Remove the Friction, Unlock the Value

Approval workflows should enable productivity, not slow it down.

Removing the dependency on the Open Task button for mobile users would:

  • Dramatically improve user experience
  • Speed up critical financial approvals
  • Increase customer satisfaction
  • Deliver immediate, measurable business value

Sometimes, the most impactful innovations aren’t new features—they’re the removal of unnecessary steps.

It’s time to let mobile approvals be truly mobile.

Dear customer,

The mentioned Instance-related notification emails for SAP SuccessFactors are cloud service alert notifications, which are sent from email address sapcloudsupport@alerts.ondemand.com. 

For such notifications, kindly know they are subscribed and managed on customer side in the Get Notified application in SAP for Me. For the detailed subscription steps, kindly refer to the following KBAs:

  • KBA 2900069 - How S-users manage their own Cloud Notification subscriptions - SAP for Me 
  • KBA 2896942 - How Super and Cloud Administrators manage Cloud Notification subscriptions for other users - SAP for Me 

I further checked your S-user, with your current S-user authorizations, you can only manage your own subscription.

If you would like all the notifications sent to another user, then only the super user or the cloud admins in your company can do it now.

Could you pls kindly access the My Important Contacts page to find your company's super users or cloud admins, then contact them to manage the subscription list per KBA 2896942?

Hope the above information helps. Any other issue, feel free to let me know.

Thanks and best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Custom Entity Hierarchy Value Help
entry.content:
Good day @Reserve 

Thank you for your question.

The answer to your proposed workaround, the answer is partly yes, but not as a true hierarchical value help.

A custom field of type Code List based on CDS View can use a value help view, and the defined value help is used in extended applications as the value help for the custom field.

3612451 - Custom Fields - Frequently Asked Questions - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/acb2907f362944a5a9e1b5eb86e769dc.html?locale=en-US

However, this option gives you a flat value help list, not a real expandable hierarchy value help. So, if your business requirement is that the user must browse parent and child nodes in a tree structure inside the value help, then this is not the right supported workaround for an app that is not released for cloud development.

This basically means that:

  • You can create a custom field if the business context of the app supports key user extensibility.
  • You can expose allowed value help data through a Code List based on CDS View.
  • You can also consider a Custom Business Object as the source and create a custom CDS value help view on top of it.
  • Note that a Code List based on CDS View will behave like a normal value help list, not like a hierarchical tree.
  • If the CDS View or data definition comes from developer extensibility and is not supported for the Custom Fields app, it may not be available or may fail when you use it.
  • Do not use unreleased application artifacts only to force a hierarchy value help.

See KBA 3422413 for details on the supported interaction between CDS views in the ABAP Environment and Code List based on CDS View.

So, my recommended approach is:

  • Use a flat code list value help if the hierarchy is only needed for classification but the user can select from a flat list.  For example, maintain values such as:

Code Description               Parent Node

A100 Europe Sales             A000

A110 Germany Sales          A100

A120 Switzerland Sales     A100

The filed remains flat, but you can include the parent node or hierarchy path as an additional descriptive field.

  • If the values are customer owned, create a Custom Business Object for the value list. Then either use the custom field type Association to Business Object or you create a Custom CDS View with the Value Help scenario on top of the Custom Business Object and use it as Code List based on CDS View. This aligns with KBA 3422413
  • If you are working in a 3-system landscape and the object is properly released and supported, a custom entity can be used for ABAP implemented value help. But this does not remove the limitation of the target application. If the app or business context does not support the field or value behaviour, it will not become a supported extension just because a custom entity exists. KBA 3612451 provides more details.
  • If the business requires a true hierarchical value help with expandable nodes, then this is not a standard key user workaround for an application that is not released for cloud development.

There is no clean-core recommendation to bypass the unreleased app restriction. You may consider raising influence request or incident to request for support.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Native Promotion Engine Crashes Due to Integer Overflow When numberOfUnits Exceeds Integer.MAX_V
entry.content:
Hello Fadel,

From a technical perspective, Long.intValue() performs a narrowing primitive conversion, which discards the upper 32 bits of the long value. When the original value exceeds Integer.MAX_VALUE (2,147,483,647), the truncated result overflows and may wrap around into a negative number. For example, converting 4,294,967,295L using intValue() results in -1.

We attempted to reproduce the issue locally using a maximum observed quantity of approximately 17,500,000,000; however, we were unable to replicate the behavior. It is unclear whether any unit conversions are being applied within your customized product configurations that could contribute to this discrepancy.

Following internal discussions, the development team has identified the following open questions:

  1. Regarding the maximum observed quantity (~17,500,000,000), could you confirm whether this value originates from a real production scenario? In typical order processing contexts, quantities exceeding 2,147,483,648 appear to be impractical. To mitigate this, we recommend using more appropriately scaled and human-readable units within promotion settings. For example, representing the quantity as 17,500 m² instead of 17,500,000,000 mm² would keep the value within the bounds of Integer.MAX_VALUE.
  2. Changing the data type for product quantities in promotion evaluation from int to long in the out-of-the-box (OOTB) implementation could introduce potential breaking changes, particularly at the code level (e.g., Java interface modifications). Therefore, we recommend that any such adjustments be handled through customer-specific customizations to ensure compatibility and controlled impact.

 

 

Kind Regards

Balaji

SAP Support

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition - your foundation for enterprise-wide agentic AI integration
entry.content:
Integration conversations with customers are changing fast.

It’s no longer about connecting systems.  It’s about enabling intelligent processes that can act, adapt, and scale.  That’s where things get interesting.

In this session, we’ll explore SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition and why it matters right now.  You’ll get a clear view of the problems it solves and how to guide customers from where they are today to something much more connected and AI-ready.

We’ll also share how to position it without overcomplicating the message.  Because sometimes, the hardest part is not the tech. It’s explaining the value in a way that clicks.

And yes, we’ll cover how agentic AI fits into all of this in a practical way.

If you’ve been looking for a clearer story to tell, this is a good place to start.

Register now and join us.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/apr/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013974.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #AgenticAI #CloudIntegration #SAPIntegrationSuite

entry.title: Calling All EHS Professionals:
entry.content:
Join Us in Potsdam for a Day and a Half That Could Change How You Think About Audits

Let's be honest: audit and inspection management doesn't always get the spotlight it deserves in EHS conversations. It sits at the heart of operational compliance, risk reduction, and continuous improvement — yet the tools, processes, and best practices around it often feel like they're playing catch-up.

That's exactly why we're bringing people together.

What's Happening

On 9–10 June 2026, we're hosting the SAP EHS Audit & Inspection Management Customer Forum at the SAP Innovation Center in Potsdam — a focused, invitation-only gathering for EHS customers and partners who work with audit and inspection processes every day.

This isn't a conference with thousands of attendees and back-to-back keynotes. It's a small, intentional format designed for real conversation — between you, your peers, and the SAP product team.

What You'll Actually Do There

Over 1.5 days, you'll move between sessions that combine strategy, product insight, and hands-on exploration:

Day 1 kicks off with a look at where audit management stands today — what challenges customers are actually running into, how different organizations are tackling them, and where SAP is heading next. There's a dedicated session on AI in Audit & Inspection Management (yes, genuinely useful AI — not just buzzwords), followed by a demo and roadmap deep-dive into Inspection Management. And of course, time to connect with peers over lunch and dinner.

Day 2 is where things get practical. Guided hands-on exercises let you work directly with the latest capabilities. Then we open it up: a feedback session with the SAP team where your voice — your requirements, your frustrations, your ideas — directly informs what gets built next.

Why It's Worth Your Time

A few things make this format different:

You meet the people building the product. Not a sales rep. Not a reseller. The actual SAP product team — who want to hear what's working, what isn't, and what you need.

You benchmark with peers across industries. Audit and inspection challenges look different in chemicals vs. manufacturing vs. energy. Getting a room full of EHS professionals together creates insights you simply can't get from a webinar.

You get early visibility. Roadmap previews, upcoming AI features, capabilities in development — you see it before it's released.

And it's free to attend. Travel and accommodation are at your own expense, but participation itself costs nothing.

Seats Are Limited

This is an invitation-only event, and spots are intentionally kept small to make the conversations meaningful. If you're managing audit or inspection processes in your organization and want a seat at the table — reach out.

We'd love to see you in Potsdam.

Questions or want to know if this is the right fit for you? Drop a comment below or send a direct message.

entry.title: Integration C4C --> S4: How to block a contact person in C4 with ERP integration correctly?
entry.content:
Hi, 

we are using C4C with S4 integration. The contact persons (CP) are managed in C4C and are replicated to S4. 

So it happens that a contact is blocked in C4C, but we see that the contact is still in the relations in S4 and can be chosen during order entry VA01 as contact person. 

Then afterwards, the order replication to C4C runs in error as the CP is not active any more. 

So I am wondering, what is the correct usage/Configuration for blocking contact persons in order to avoid these errors? What is the best practice setup here?

Thx, chris

entry.title: Control execution of subsequent actions based on Labor On result in a single POD button
entry.content:
I would like to confirm whether the following behavior can be controlled when using a single action button in POD 1.0.

Scenario:
I assign the following to a single button:
1. Labor On plugin
2. Production Process A

Requirement:
If the Labor On step does not complete successfully, I want to prevent the subsequent Production Process A from being executed.
Specifically, I want to stop the flow in cases such as:
・The operator presses the Cancel button in the Labor On dialog
・An invalid (non-existent) badge ID is entered

Question:
Is it possible to conditionally stop the execution of subsequent actions (e.g., Production Process A) based on the result of the Labor On plugin within a single button configuration?

If not supported out-of-the-box, are there recommended approaches (e.g., custom plugin, script task, or process design patterns) to achieve this behavior?

Thank you.

entry.title: Replay: Mastering global e-invoicing complexities
entry.content:
The global invoicing landscape is moving rapidly as new mandates drive the move towards more transparent, standardised, real-time e-invoicing. As part of the last Network Success Day, a breakout session focused on the current situation, the upcoming mandates and how SAP Business Network plans on addressing them. The session reiterated SAP's e-invoicing strategy, and presented the solutions put in place for the recent mandates in Belgium and Poland, and for the upcoming mandate in France.

This recording summarizes in just 18 minutes the presentation given by three SAP experts who broke down the regulatory changes and mandates that matter most, highlighted how SAP Business Network is supporting global compliance, and how new innovations will add value for all trading partners, buyers and sellers alike.

 

 

Make sure to pay a regular visit to our SAP Community page for suppliers to get the latest updates on e-invoicing compliance and more!

entry.title: Re: CLM Lease-out revenue postings on cost center / wbs-e...
entry.content:
Good day @JFPC 

Thank you for your question.

In the lease contract account assignment, the supported controlling assignment options are Cost Center or WBS Element. You can select either cost centers or WBS elements as account assignments.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/93bd0b1e72ca4cbcbfb942d4497529f7/a3714d581b2443d7ae1056694a8c877e.html?locale=en-US 

So, to answer your question, it is not possible to post the lease-out revenue directly to only a Profit Center or directly to a Profitability Segment without using the supported CLM account assignment object. See KBA 3533273 on the profit center derivation behaviour for journal entry postings.

This basically means:

  • The lease-out contract itself is not used as a standalone controlling object in the same way as in the previous RE-FX setup.
  • The standard CLM account assignment options are Cost Center or WBS Element.
  • Profit Center is not entered as the primary CLM contract account assignment.
  • Profit Center is derived through the assigned cost center or WBS element.
  • Direct posting to a profitability segment from the lease-out contract is not available as a standard CLM account assignment option.
  • If a cost center is assigned, the posting may appear as a negative cost posting on a P&L account but the profit center reporting should still be driven by the profit center derived from that cost center.
  • If WBS is used, the WBS can carry the required profit center and can support more project-based analysis.

See the following references for more details.

3708952 - Error F5808 - 'Field Cost Ctr is required for G/L account' during Periodic or Valuation posting of lease contract - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

3533273 - Profit center cannot be changed while posting journal entries, information message KI339 occurs - SAP for Me

2692119 - SAP Contract and Lease Management (SAP RE-FX) - Consulting notes - SAP for Me

The clean approach i recommend is to use one of the supported objects and derive the profit center from it.

Option 1 is to create or use a dedicated cost center that represents the leas-out object, building, property, or profit responsibility. Maintain the correct profit center in the cost center master data. Then assign this cost center to the contract object. This does not make the cost center the final reporting objective. It acts as the required controlling assignment from which the profit center is derived.

Option 2, if your customer wants contract, building, or rental object level reporting, a WBS element is often cleaner than a generic cost center. The WBS can be structured to represent the leased object and assigned to the required profit center. Use this option where the business wants better tracking by property, lease object, investment object, or business unit.

Alternatively, as option 3, if your customer’s real requirement is reporting by profit center, then keep the standard CLM account assignment and report the revenue through the derived profit center in G/L reporting. Use apps such as Display Line Items in General Ledger or profit center reporting to validate the resulting postings.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: What's New in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 Analytics - April 2026
entry.content:
As part of our Q1 2026 releases, SAP continues to strengthen analytics capabilities across SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management. These enhancements reflect a dual focus:

  • Expanding extensibility through partner and customer-driven innovations
  • Improving system stability, resilience, and data reliability

Our releases in the first quarter of 2026 introduce four key innovations designed to deliver tangible business value:

  • Analytics Support for Custom Service Entities (Restricted GA)
  • Where-Used Visibility for Extension Fields
  • Survey Data Source for Response Analytics
  • Automatic Data Resynchronization

Below is a closer look at each enhancement.

Analytics Support for Custom Service Entities (Restricted GA)

Modern enterprises require flexibility to tailor solutions to their unique processes. Both SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management support extensibility through Custom Services, enabling customers and partners to extend standard functionality.

With this release, analytics now fully supports these custom service entities within Embedded SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC).

What’s new?

  • Automatic creation of a “Custom” data source for eligible service entities
  • Seamless availability in the Embedded SAC Query Designer
  • Ability to combine custom and standard data sources for richer insights

Key prerequisites

  • Custom service entity must be marked as Analytics Relevant
  • Required attributes must also be flagged accordingly

For more information on how to setup a custom service for analytics, please refer our online documentation here & here.

Business value

  • Enables reporting on fully customized business processes
  • Eliminates dependency on only standard data models
  • Supports advanced analytics scenarios with combined datasets

This feature is currently available under Restricted General Availability. Customers can request access via an incident on component CEC-CRM-ANA, with broader rollout planned soon.

Where-Used Visibility for Extension Fields

Managing extension fields can be challenging, especially when assessing downstream dependencies.

What’s new?

Admins can now view a Where-Used list for extension fields directly within Extensibility Administration.

Why it matters

  • Identifies whether a field is used in Embedded SAC stories
  • Prevents accidental deletion that could break reports
  • Enables safer system cleanup and optimization

Business value

This seemingly small enhancement significantly reduces risk and improves governance in extensibility management.

Survey Data Source for Response Analytics

Customer feedback is critical—but until now, analysing actual survey responses within analytics tools was limited.

What’s new?

A dedicated “Survey” standard data source is now available.

Previously

  • Only survey metadata and design-level data sources existed
  • No direct way to analyse submitted responses

Now

  • Full access to survey response data
  • Ability to build SAC stories for deeper analysis

Business value

  • Enables insight into customer sentiment and feedback trends
  • Supports data-driven decision-making based on real responses
  • Bridges a long-standing analytics gap highlighted by customers

Automatic Data Resynchronization

SAP’s cloud-native architecture relies on distributed microservices, each optimized for its own purpose. While this ensures scalability and performance, it introduces complexity in analytics.

The challenge

  • Analytics data is replicated into SAP HANA via event-driven mechanisms
  • With high event volumes, rare inconsistencies can occur between:
    • Source microservices
    • Analytics data store (HANA)

What’s new?

A new intelligent background job automatically:

  • Detects inconsistencies between primary and analytics data stores
  • Corrects mismatches proactively
  • Schedules execution to minimize system impact

Business value

  • Ensures data accuracy and consistency in Embedded SAC
  • Builds trust in analytics outputs
  • Maintains system performance while improving reliability

Closing Thoughts

This quarter’s innovations emphasize two critical pillars: extensibility and data reliability.

By enabling analytics on custom entities, improving governance, unlocking survey insights, and ensuring data consistency, SAP continues to enhance the value of Embedded SAC within its cloud solutions.

These capabilities empower organizations to:

  • Gain deeper, more relevant insights
  • Operate with confidence in their data
  • Extend analytics to match evolving business needs

We look forward to seeing how customers leverage these enhancements to drive smarter, data-informed decisions in 2026 and beyond.

entry.title: Zero Amount Invoice in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Professional Services
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are working in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – Professional Services, using Manage Project Billing.

We have a requirement to issue a customer invoice with zero amount, linked to a professional services project/WBS element.

Business case

We need to document a donation of 15 IT devices:

  • Tax base: 0.00 EUR
  • VAT: 0.00 EUR
  • Total invoice amount: 0.00 EUR
  • The customer tax classification is maintained as Exempt Spain 0%

Current behavior

When trying to create the billing item through Manage Project Billing, the system raises this error:

Billing plan item with zero amount not allowed

Because of this, we cannot continue with the project billing process or create the invoice.

Questions

  1. Is this standard behavior in Professional Services / Manage Project Billing?
  2. Is there any standard configuration or extensibility option to allow zero-amount project billing?
  3. Has anyone used a workaround while keeping the transaction linked to the project/WBS element?

Any guidance would be appreciated.

Thank you.
